1: Where is your mother's bill of divorce, with which I sent her away? Or which of my creditors is it to whom I have sold you? Behold, for your iniquities you were sold, and for your transgressions your mother was sent away. YHWH opens the Third Servant Song by establishing that Israel's exile was self-inflicted — no divorce certificate, no creditor's sale. The covenant relationship, though fractured by Israel's sin, was never abandoned by YHWH. John 1:11: he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. The Servant who speaks in vv4-9 comes into the very situation this verse describes — not a deserted wife but a people who deserted YHWH. The Incarnation is YHWH not accepting the separation but coming himself to restore what human sin broke.
2: Why, when I came, was there no man? Why, when I called, was there no one to answer? Is my hand shortened, that it cannot redeem? Or have I no power to deliver? Behold, by my rebuke I dry up the sea, I make the rivers a desert. YHWH came and found no one. John 1:11: he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. The arm that is not too short to redeem (Exodus 6:6: I will redeem you with an outstretched arm) is identified in Isaiah 53:1 as the arm of the LORD revealed in the Servant. The power to dry the sea (the Exodus) is the same power exercised by Jesus who rebukes the storm (Mark 4:39: peace, be still!) — the creator's arm in covenant action, coming to redeem though met with silence.
3: I clothe the heavens with blackness and make sackcloth their covering. Creation's mourning-garment of darkness — the cosmic dimension of divine grief over covenant rupture. The darkening of the heavens finds its NT fulfillment in the crucifixion: Luke 23:44-45 records that darkness came over the whole land from the sixth to the ninth hour, and the sun's light failed. The heavenly sackcloth of Isaiah 50:3 is the darkness of Golgotha — creation itself donning mourning clothes as the one through whom it was made bears its sin.
4: The Lord GOD has given me the tongue of those who are taught, that I may know how to sustain with a word him who is weary. Morning by morning he awakens; he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught. The Servant speaks as a disciple — the tongue of the learned, the ear awakened daily. The content of the Servant's speech is sustaining the weary: Matthew 11:28-30: Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Hebrews 5:8-9: although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered; and being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation. Jesus, the eternal Son, took on the form of a disciple — learning, growing, sustained morning by morning by the Father's word.
5: The Lord GOD has opened my ear, and I was not rebellious; I turned not backward. The Servant's perfect obedience contrasted with Israel's persistent rebellion (the entire preceding chapters). Philippians 2:8: and being found in human form, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Hebrews 5:8: although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered. In Gethsemane Jesus expresses the ultimate form of the opened ear: not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42). The non-rebellious, non-turning Servant is the one who kept his face set toward Jerusalem even when all others fled.
6: I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting. The most direct Passion prophecy before chapter 53 — each detail fulfilled with precision. Matthew 26:67: they spat in his face and struck him. And some slapped him. Mark 14:65: some began to spit on him and to cover his face and to strike him. Matthew 27:26: Pilate having scourged Jesus, delivered him to be crucified. The giving of the back to the smiters, the cheeks to those who pull the beard, the face to spitting and disgrace — the Passion narratives fulfill Isaiah 50:6 with such specificity that the disciples would have recognized the echo immediately after Pentecost.
7: But the Lord GOD helps me; therefore I have not been disgraced; therefore I have set my face like a flint, and I know that I shall not be put to shame. The Servant's resolve — face set like flint — is the language Luke uses for Jesus's final journey: Luke 9:51: when the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem. This is a direct lexical echo of Isaiah 50:7's set my face like flint. The certainty of divine help grounds the Servant's determination; the certainty of resurrection grounds Jesus's determination. He sets his face because he knows what the Father will do — the shame absorbed at the cross will not be the final word.
8: He who vindicates me is near. Who will contend with me? Let us stand up together. Who is my adversary? Let him come near to me. The Servant's legal confidence in divine vindication. Romans 8:33-34 applies this directly to the risen Christ: Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us. Paul uses the Servant's courtroom-language of Isaiah 50:8 to describe the position of the risen Christ before the divine court — and by extension the legal status of all who are in him.
9: Behold, the Lord GOD helps me; who will declare me guilty? Behold, all of them will wear out like a garment; the moth will eat them up. The Servant's accusers will wear out while he endures. Hebrews 1:10-12 uses the garment-wearing-out image for the creation that passes away while the Son remains unchanged: they will wear out like a garment... but you are the same, and your years will have no end. The Servant's legal vindication — no one can declare him guilty — becomes the doctrine of Christ's sinlessness: 2 Corinthians 5:21 — him who knew no sin. His accusers (Caiaphas, the Sanhedrin, Pilate) have been consumed while the one they condemned is still worshipped.
10: Who among you fears the LORD and obeys the voice of his servant? Let him who walks in darkness and has no light trust in the name of the LORD and rely on his God. The covenant community is now defined by obedience to the Servant's voice. John 10:27: my sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. Hebrews 1:2: in these last days God has spoken to us by his Son. The walking-in-darkness person who trusts in YHWH's name is the person who trusts in the name of Jesus: Acts 4:12 — there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved. The Servant's voice is now the voice of Christ.
11: Behold, all you who kindle a fire, who equip yourselves with burning torches! Walk by the light of your fire, and by the torches that you have kindled! This you have from my hand: you shall lie down in torment. The warning to those who choose self-generated light over YHWH's provision. John 8:12: I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life. The self-kindled fire is the human achievement-system: human reason, human righteousness, human religion manufactured without the Servant. Revelation 20:14-15: the lake of fire is the endpoint of those who chose their own light. The torment that comes from one's own hand (the fire returns on its kindlers) is the ironic justice of rejecting the Light of the world for self-made illumination.
